tiny_practice.action: Close your eyes and imagine running from a responsibility God has given you. Take three deep breaths, acknowledging where you might be avoiding God's call.

two_step.reflect_question: What current assignment from God are you potentially trying to escape?

options.response: Schedule a meeting or take first step of obedience this week
Pray for courage and set up the conversation
Journal about fears and seek godly counsel

creative_prompt.instruction: Draw a picture of a boat being tossed by a storm, representing moments when God gets your attention

reevaluation.question: How has reading Jonah 1 changed your understanding of God's pursuit?
options: God is more patient than I realized
God will use any circumstance to communicate
Running from God always creates complications

three_choice.question: Which perspective on divine calling resonates most?

choices.expansion: Jonah demonstrates how human fear and personal preference can override spiritual assignment.
Every attempt to flee divine purpose ultimately creates more complicated circumstances.
God uses unexpected events to redirect and ultimately communicate His larger intentions.

socratic_nudge.question: When you perceive a challenging divine instruction, what internal mechanisms make you hesitate or resist?

moral_slider.question: How do personal comfort and spiritual obedience intersect in your life?

reevaluation.question: How has your understanding of divine guidance evolved?
options: I see divine instructions as invitations, not commands

thematic_summaries.title: Divine Sovereignty and Human Rebellion
thematic_summaries.summary: God's call to Jonah demonstrates His sovereign authority over human choices. Despite Jonah's attempt to flee, God's purposes cannot be thwarted by human resistance.

thematic_summaries.title: Providence in Judgment and Mercy
thematic_summaries.summary: God uses unexpected circumstances and people to accomplish His redemptive purposes. The storm and sailors' reactions reveal God's broader plan of drawing people to understanding.

reevaluation.question: How does Jonah's initial response reflect potential theological misunderstandings about God's mission and compassion?
options: Nationalistic prejudice limiting God's redemptive scope
Fear of personal discomfort in challenging assignments
Misunderstanding God's universal love for all peoples
Resistance to prophetic calling and spiritual accountability
